tickets.title: Primitive byte arrays with a MIME type: a bytes Target kind (0.4, additive)
tickets.body:
## Proposal

Add an **additive** `Target` kind for raw binary payloads with an attached MIME type:

```ts
{ kind: "bytes", mime: string, value: Uint8Array }
```

- **Identity is the hash of the raw bytes.** Encoding is transport, never identity: base64url in JSON wire contexts, a native byte string in CBOR. This removes base64's canonicalization hazards (variant alphabets, padding) from the content address entirely.
- **MIME rides in-kind**, RDF-datatype style — attested interpretation-testimony that travels with the bytes, not a detachable sibling pointer. Two claims asserting the same bytes under different MIME types are different claims, which is honest.

## Motivation

Loam is converging on a doctrine of *code and documents shipped as deltas*: a delta asserts the content-addressed bytes of a coherent, versioned unit, and the signature attests exactly the bytes that run (Loam TODO, "The snapshot doctrine"). Text (ESM source) works today as a string primitive. But the renderer work coming in Loam §23 — UI components pushed as deltas, mounted by a stock host — wants **basic binary assets right in the hypergraph**: icons, small images, fonts, wasm. Today those mean base64-in-a-string: ~4/3 bloat, no typing, and an encoding sitting inside the content address.

A `bytes` primitive also gives every downstream surface what it needs: a REST door can answer with a correct `Content-Type`, an inspector can render "a 43 KB `image/png`" instead of a base64 wall, and sandbox/quarantine machinery gets a signal about what kind of thing it is holding.

## Compatibility

Additive, so the story is clean:

- **No migration.** Existing deltas do not change; old stores simply never contain the new kind. The new shape is unambiguously distinct from all prior versions by construction (a new `kind`), so shape-detection can never misfire.
- **Federation forward-compat**: a pre-0.4 peer pulling from a store holding `bytes` deltas refuses the unknown kind. That is version discipline, not breakage — and admission shape-requirements can express it explicitly.

## Non-goals / cautions

- **This is not a blob store.** A 10 MB image in a delta stresses every path — hyperview copies, federation offers, a browser peer's ~5 MB origin quota. The primitive is the *inline* rung of a storage ladder (inline bytes → content-addressed ref → chunked tree); large assets still climb the ladder, and doors may cap sizes as deployment config. The primitive's job is to make the inline rung honest and the reference rungs typed.
- The delta grammar stays small; this adds one kind, not a media subsystem.

## Open questions for the design

1. Exact canonical form: where the raw bytes enter the hash preimage, and the pinned JSON-wire encoding (base64url, unpadded?).
2. Conformance vectors: hash-stability vectors for the new kind across the TS and Rust implementations.
3. Any size guidance the substrate itself should state (likely none — a caps-are-deployment-config note may be enough).
4. Whether `mime` is required or defaults to `application/octet-stream`.

Proposed as rhizomatic 0.4, timed alongside Loam's §23 (renderers) design stage; nothing in Loam §21/§22 blocks on it.

🤖 Drafted with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) at Myk's request.

tickets.title: Add a Schema Schema (`SCHEMA_SCHEMA`) — self-hosting parity for the resolution Schema
tickets.body:
rhizomatic ships `HYPER_SCHEMA_SCHEMA` — the self-hosting definition of a **HyperSchema** — plus `publishSchemaClaims`/`loadSchema` to publish and load one as deltas. There is **no equivalent for the resolution `Schema`** (`{ props: Map<field, Policy>, default: Policy }`): no `SCHEMA_SCHEMA`, no at-rest delta vocabulary for a Schema, and the `Schema` type carries no name. A Schema today exists only inline (canonical JSON quoted inside a consumer's registration), never as a first-class, resolvable domain node.

**Why this is needed.** Loam's §21 (schema identity & versioning) makes a Schema a first-class entity — its own id, its own deltas, resolved from the ground exactly as a HyperSchema is — with registration demoted to a binding, so that many named lenses can live over one HyperSchema and each can be snapshotted into a pinnable `name@hash` version. That requires publishing/loading a Schema *as deltas, resolved by a Schema Schema*. The whole system rests on the symmetry `HyperSchema : HyperView :: Schema : View`; if one side of it has a self-hosting schema-schema and publish/load, the other side deserves the parallel for the identical reasons — self-description, portability, resolvability. Doing it in Loam instead would fork the substrate's self-hosting story at the application layer (a parallel `loam.schema.*`), exactly the near-synonym duplication the vocabulary discipline avoids.

**Proposed.** Add `SCHEMA_SCHEMA` (the self-hosting definition of a resolution Schema) and publish/load parity — either `publishSchemaClaims`/`loadSchema` equivalents for Schemas, or a generalization of the existing pair — with an at-rest delta vocabulary (`rhizomatic.schema.*`, parallel to `rhizomatic.hyperschema.*`).

**Coordination.** This blocks Loam §21, which already ships an on-wire migration (its `schema:`→`hyperschema:` entity rename). Ideally this lands in the **same release** as the naming reconciliation in #10, so Loam pulls one new rhizomatic and folds everything into §21's single migration wave rather than cutting a second one.

**A deeper question, noted not blocked (Myk).** Whether the query/resolution apparatus (schema-schemas, Policies, `resolveView`) is fundamentally substrate, or a separable layer above the truly-portable data format (deltas, content-addressing, signatures, negation), is a real long-horizon architectural question — one could imagine the format being the substrate and the query layer living above it. But the HyperSchema Schema already lives in rhizomatic, so for consistency the Schema Schema joining it is the smaller, parity-preserving move; the separability question can be its own discussion.

Referenced by Loam §21 (schema identity); related to #10 (the `loadSchema`/`publishSchemaClaims` naming lag).

tickets.title: Vocabulary: `loadSchema` / `publishSchemaClaims` operate on HyperSchemas, not Schemas
tickets.body:
Since the 0.3.0 L5 realignment, the at-rest schema-definition vocabulary is `rhizomatic.hyperschema.*`, and the conceptual split is **HyperSchema** (the gather program) vs **Schema** (the resolution program: `{ props: Map<field, Policy>, default: Policy }`). But the public API `loadSchema` and `publishSchemaClaims` load and publish **HyperSchemas** — the names predate the realignment and now lag their meaning.

This surfaced while designing Loam's §21 (schema identity & versioning), which makes a Loam-level Schema a first-class entity distinct from the HyperSchema. Loam is renaming its own `schema:` entity prefix to remove the blur on its side, but the substrate API names remain a source of confusion: a reader of `loadSchema` reasonably expects it to load a Schema (a resolution program), when it in fact loads a HyperSchema (a gather program).

rhizomatic is frozen/normative, so this is filed for discussion, not as a Loam workaround. Options:
- **(a)** rename to `loadHyperSchema` / `publishHyperSchemaClaims` — breaking API change (conformance vectors + version bump + migration guidance);
- **(b)** add the accurate names as aliases and deprecate the old ones;
- **(c)** document the lag and keep the names as stable API even though the vocabulary moved under them.

No urgency — Loam routes around it in prose today (§21 records the seam). Raising per Myk's request to resolve the last naming detail.

_Filed from the Loam §21 design review._

edges.contract: the settled loadSchema/publishSchemaClaims API naming that #11 designs its Schema publish/load parity against

tickets.title: Composable set-difference (and intersection) term operators over delta-sets
tickets.body:
## Summary

Complete the first-class **set algebra over delta-sets**: `Term` has `union` but no `difference` (minus) or `intersect`. Add composable `difference` and `intersect` term operators over `dset`-sort terms, symmetric with `union`.

## Motivation (from Loam's container work, SPEC §24/§27)

Loam is building **containers**: a container's *membership* is a delta-query — a `Term` that evaluates to a `dset` (exactly the shape `offeredLens` already uses). Organizing a store into containers, some **excluded** (the "sandbox" property), needs the read scope to be *"the union of the active containers **minus** the excluded ones"* — i.e. **set algebra over delta-sets**: `∪`, `∩`, `∖`.

Today the `Term` algebra (`src/eval.ts` `Term` union) has **`union` as a first-class op, but not difference or intersection**. That asymmetry is the gap: you can compose containers by union but not by exclusion or overlap, which are exactly what "some containers are sandboxed/excluded" and "what's shared between two containers" require.

## Current state (what I verified)

The one way to express exclusion today is the reflective `inView` predicate + `not`:

```
select{
  pred: not( inView{ term: T, field: "id", extract: { kind: "field", field: "id" } } ),
  of: input
}
```

= `input ∖ T` (by delta id). This works for the simple single-level case, but three constraints stop it from being the general set-difference the container model needs:

1. **Depth-1 stratification** (`inView.term` may not itself contain `inView` — enforced at parse). So you **cannot difference against a term that is itself a difference** — i.e. containers defined *relative to other containers* (A = base ∖ B, where B = base ∖ C) don't compose. This is the blocker for composable/nested containers.
2. **`dset`-sort only** for the referenced term.
3. **Keyed only on `id`/`author`** (the two `inView.field` options), not a general set operation over the whole delta.

`union` has none of these limits — it's a plain, nestable, whole-delta term op. Difference/intersection should match it.

## Requested

Two new `Term` ops, symmetric with `union`, over `dset`-sort operands, composable/nestable to any depth (whole-delta, deduped by content-addressed id):

```ts
| { readonly kind: "difference"; readonly of: Term; readonly without: Term }   // of.set ∖ without.set
| { readonly kind: "intersect";  readonly left: Term; readonly right: Term }   // left.set ∩ right.set
```

`difference` is the concretely-blocking one (exclusion / the sandbox property). `intersect` completes the boolean set algebra (you have `∪`; `∖` and `∩` finish it) and covers overlapping containers ("what's in both A and B").

## Scope / non-goals

Deliberately narrow. This is **not** asking for regex/substring string matching, bytes-target (mime/payload) predicates, a delta-intrinsic context/role, or `between` on the timestamp field — the existing `match` (author/timestamp/id with the `Cmp` vocabulary), `hasPointer`, and `and`/`or`/`not` already cover container-membership selection (authored-by, time-range, explicit-id-set, touching-entity, in-context, boolean combos all verified expressible). The single gap is composable **set difference** (and its natural companion, intersection).

## Context

Design-stage request for discussion, tied to Loam's §24 quarantine / §27 containers work (membership = a delta-query; sandbox = a container property; the scope is set algebra over containers). Happy to iterate on the shape — the essential need is *composable, nestable set-difference over dsets*, first-class like `union`.
